text: I live on a boat in a harbour that has tiding (if that is the correct word for the water going up and down by influence of the moon) So there is always waterflow around my boat. author: @HolePuncherStudios
text: Robert,  I love your videos.  I am of the philosophy that technology is the one thing that improves human life above all else.  Your in-depth knowledge of the physical world makes me hopeful for the future. I believe that we are surrounded by an almost limitless amount of untapped energy and will one day unlock cheap, if not free , energy for all.  Thank you sharing your information with the world. Btw, are you familiar with the futurist Jaques Fresco and his ideas of a society guided by the scientific Method and a resource based economy?

author: @sinenomine9093
text: From what I remember about crossflow turbines the entrance channel, jet, lip or whatever you want to call it is important to maximum efficiency. It stands to reason that you would want the flow to be as laminar as possible.  And ideally, you want the same to be true of the water striking the exit turbine vans as well to extract as much of the remaining momentum while exiting.

replies.text: i thas been done with and without - you get an improvement either way but you ar right it is best with the lip

author: @jamest.5001
text: Sounds much more efficient than a undershot water wheel!.

author: @jamest.5001
text: This seems to be perfect for low fall  creeks. Just build a dam about 12"-24" with a spill way over it. Place the turbine at the end of the spill Way.  Only a foot or so away, or longer for larger turbines, it would be simple to build a slip roller from 2" thick wall pipe.  6 Bearings, and a chain couple sprockets, a windshield wiper motor. Or hand crank. Set the distance between the rollers to determine the diameter of the rolled sheet metal. A 2 mm to 3 mm mild steel should be plenty. Roll it to a roughly 12" to 36" or larger diameter. Depending on the area used in.  Make sections in 12"'24" 48" cut end caps. And brace rings could be just 1" slip roll rings welded one inside one outside with 1/4" bar attaching the two.  Possibly going on to the center axel . Then basically put it in a box.  Mount a row of magnets on the end caps and a pair of serpentine coils on each end rectify and parallel or series to get the voltage range needed... Sounds much better than a large undershot water wheel. Lots less material and output

Sounds like you are retired. That would make you a member of the Old Farts Project. There’s lots of us retired old farts who have tons of experience, and we should pass it on.

author: @makingd.o.123
text: I wonder if you could put something like this on top of a chimney. To use the wasted heat to generate electricity in the winter for people off grid heating with wood stoves sense it's harder to use water in freezing temperatures. Might as well use a lose for a gain

replies.author: @freeforester1717
replies.text: Living in the U.K. even at 57° North (think Churchill, halfway up Hudson Bay for a North American equivalent), our nearby stream never freezes over (fast flowing water very seldom does), it should be possible to harness the energy in this way. 😊

replies.author: @makingd.o.123
replies.text: @freeforester1717 I've been trying to think about harnessing stored water for those not close to a running stream or spring. Water is already stored so might as well use it by pumping it to a higher level using it free fall or as a gravity weight but I haven't been able to think my way around freezing in winter so when I watched this video I begin thinking about using this turbine in line with the heat loss of the chimney most off grid people heat with wood already and fuel is fuel might as well get everything you can out of it

replies.author: @gerryplayz4532
replies.text: You can get a "back boiler" for stoves or open fires to heat radiators, I think you could hook it up to some sort of steam engine. I saw a video where the guy built a giant sterling engine, if you're somewhere cold the condenser part could be outside and your woodstove would be the burner!?

author: @billschwandt1
text: Shape the inlet like half of a hyperbolic funnel and let the water flow over the flat part and funnel into the wheel. You will get more power this way because of the shape of the water. Separately, we went over a patent last night from Russia that uses cavitation to generate power very far over unity. I wrote a paper about potential energy and how it applies to the cavitation tech exhibited in the patent over on my subs tack. I think you could really be helpful with what is going on the Martin fleischman memorial channel. It's small but the research is very important. You should check it out!

author: @jimreynolds3798
text: You remind me of my stepfather. He was an uneducated mechanical and electrical genius. He repaired everything from cars to CB’s and tv’s. He could even rebuild watches and clocks, and had thousands of parts to do it with. Well done sir!!!
